title: How to Get a SIREN/SIRET Number as a Freelancer in France

interlinking_suggestions.title: Sole Proprietorship vs. LLC: The Definitive Guide for Global Freelancers

A Freelancer's Guide to Keyword Research

author.name: Maria Kowalski
author.bio: Based in Berlin, Maria helps non-EU freelancers navigate the complexities of the European market. She's an expert on VAT, EU-specific invoicing requirements, and business registration across different EU countries.

reviewedBy.name: Priya Singh, Esq.
reviewedBy.bio: Priya is an attorney specializing in international contract law for independent contractors. She ensures that the legal advice provided is accurate, actionable, and up-to-date with current regulations.
credentials: Member of the New York State Bar

key_takeaways: Verify your long-stay visa or residence permit explicitly grants the right to self-employed work in France before beginning any part of the business registration process.
Use only the official and free 'Guichet unique' INPI portal for registration, as the assignment of a SIREN/SIRET number is a no-cost government procedure.
To maintain legal compliance, ensure every invoice includes your 14-digit SIRET number and the mandatory phrase 'TVA non applicable, art. 293 B du CGI' if you are not charging VAT.
File your revenue declarations with URSSAF on your chosen schedule (monthly or quarterly), even if your income is zero for the period, to avoid penalties for non-compliance.
summary: For global professionals, the anxiety of navigating French bureaucracy is a major barrier to legally launching a business, as a SIREN/SIRET number is mandatory for invoicing and securing corporate clients. This guide provides a clear three-stage playbook, advising you to first complete a pre-application checklist to mitigate risks before executing a flawless submission on the official INPI portal. By following this methodical process, you can confidently obtain your free registration numbers, establishing the professional legitimacy needed to build client trust and operate as a compliant French business entity.
ai_overview_answer: To get a SIREN/SIRET number in France, you must register your business through the official INPI "Guichet unique" online portal, a process which is completely free. Before applying, you must confirm your legal right to work in a self-employed capacity, secure a physical French address, and choose a business structure, with "micro-entreprise" being the most common for solo professionals. The application requires a clear description of your business activity and scanned copies of key documents, including proof of identity and address. After submitting a complete application, you can expect to receive your 9-digit SIREN (business identifier) and 14-digit SIRET (establishment identifier) within two to four weeks. This SIRET number is a legal requirement on all invoices and signals your professional legitimacy to clients. Operating without a SIRET is considered undeclared work and carries significant penalties. Upon registration, you must begin making mandatory revenue declarations to URSSAF to remain compliant.

itemListElement.name: How to Get a SIREN/SIRET Number as a Freelancer in France

@graph.name: How to Get a SIREN/SIRET Number as a Freelancer in France
@graph.description: For global professionals, the anxiety of navigating French bureaucracy is a major barrier to legally launching a business, as a SIREN/SIRET number is mandatory for invoicing and securing corporate clients. This guide provides a clear three-stage playbook, advising you to first complete a pre-application checklist to mitigate risks before executing a flawless submission on the official INPI portal. By following this methodical process, you can confidently obtain your free registration numbers, establishing the professional legitimacy needed to build client trust and operate as a compliant French business entity.

step.name: Stage 1: Your Pre-Flight Checklist—Mitigating Risk Before You Apply

itemListElement.name: Confirm Your 'Right to Work': The Non-Negotiable First Step
itemListElement.text: Before investing a moment in the application, verify your legal foundation. For a non-EU Global Professional, this means holding a valid long-stay visa or residence permit (Titre de Séjour) that explicitly grants the right to work in a self-employed capacity. A visa marked 'Entrepreneur/Profession Libérale' is typically required. Attempting to register without this authorization is a non-starter that will halt your progress immediately.

itemListElement.name: Choose Your Legal Structure: Why 'Micro-Entreprise' is Your Default
itemListElement.text: For the vast majority of solo professionals, the micro-entreprise is the most direct and logical path. This structure is designed for simplicity, allowing you to pay social charges and income tax based on revenue, not complex profit calculations. To qualify, your annual turnover must remain below specific thresholds (for 2025: €188,700 for sales; €77,700 for services and liberal professions).

itemListElement.name: Secure Your French Address (Domiciliation): The 'Where' of Your Business
itemListElement.text: You cannot register a business in France without a physical French address. This domiciliation is a legal requirement that will form a key part of your business identity, determining the final five digits of your 14-digit SIRET number. You have several valid options: Your residential address, a co-working space, or a registered office service (société de domiciliation).

itemListElement.name: Define Your Business Activity (APE Code): The 'What' That Shapes Your Future
itemListElement.text: When you register, you must clearly describe your primary business activity in French. Based on this, the national statistics office (INSEE) will assign you an APE code (Activité Principale Exercée). This five-character code is more than a label; it influences which regulations apply to your business and can impact your social security contribution rates. Be precise in your description to prevent future administrative headaches and ensure your business is correctly classified from day one.

step.name: Stage 2: Flawless Execution—Navigating the INPI Application with Confidence

itemListElement.name: Mastering the 'Guichet unique': A Step-by-Step Walkthrough
itemListElement.text: All new business registration in France is handled through a single online portal called the Guichet unique (Single Window), managed by the INPI. This centralization simplifies the process. When you begin your application as a micro-entrepreneur, you will select 'Entrepreneur individuel' as your business type.

itemListElement.name: Avoiding Common Application Errors
itemListElement.text: Simple documentary mistakes are the single biggest cause of delays. Have these key documents scanned, correctly formatted, and ready to upload: Proof of Identity (Pièce d'identité), Proof of Address (Justificatif de domicile), and a Declaration of No Criminal Conviction (Attestation de non-condamnation).

itemListElement.name: Understanding the Timeline and Cost
itemListElement.text: The official registration of your business and the assignment of your SIREN and SIRET numbers by INSEE is completely free. Be wary of third-party websites that demand a fee for what is a free government procedure. Once you submit your complete application via the INPI portal, you can expect to receive your official SIREN and SIRET numbers within two to four weeks.

step.name: Stage 3: Your Post-Activation Protocol—Putting Your SIRET Number to Work

itemListElement.name: The Anatomy of a Bulletproof French Invoice
itemListElement.text: Your 14-digit SIRET number is a non-negotiable component of every invoice you issue. Its inclusion is a matter of legal compliance and a powerful signal of professionalism. A compliant invoice must include your identity (with SIRET), client identity, core details, VAT mention, and payment terms.

itemListElement.name: Signaling Legitimacy to High-Value Clients
itemListElement.text: In the European corporate world, compliance is currency. Proactively providing your SIRET number in a proposal or contract immediately frames you as a serious, legitimate business entity. This simple act builds a foundation of trust before work begins.

itemListElement.name: Understanding Your New Compliance Rhythm
itemListElement.text: Receiving your SIRET number activates a predictable rhythm of administrative duties. You must declare your revenue to URSSAF, the social security collections body, either monthly or quarterly. This is mandatory even if your income for a period is zero. You will also be subject to the CFE Tax (Cotisation Foncière des Entreprises), an annual local business tax, though you are exempt in your first calendar year.

itemListElement.name: Verifying Your Business Publicly
itemListElement.text: Your business is now part of the official public record. You—and your clients—can verify your details on the government-run Annuaire des Entreprises (Company Directory) by searching your name or SIREN number. We recommend you do this as soon as you receive your number to confirm your official listing is accurate and professional from day one.

mainEntity.name: Can a foreigner get a SIRET number in France?

acceptedAnswer.text: Absolutely. Obtaining a SIRET number is not restricted by nationality but is entirely dependent on your legal right to reside and work in France. You must first have the correct visa or residence permit (Titre de Séjour) that allows self-employment. Once your legal status is secure, the business registration process is identical to that for a French citizen.

mainEntity.name: How much does it cost to get a SIRET number?

acceptedAnswer.text: The assignment of your SIREN and SIRET numbers by INSEE is completely free. After you submit your business creation file through the official INPI portal, your numbers are issued automatically. Be cautious of third-party services that charge fees for this free, official procedure.

mainEntity.name: What is the penalty for not having a SIRET number in France?

acceptedAnswer.text: Operating a business without a SIRET number is considered undeclared work (travail dissimulé), a serious offense. Penalties can include substantial fines (up to €45,000 for an individual) and the required back-payment of all avoided social security contributions with penalties. Furthermore, you cannot legally issue a compliant invoice, which prevents you from getting paid by legitimate clients.

mainEntity.name: Where do I put the SIRET number on an invoice?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your 14-digit SIRET number is mandatory on every invoice. It must be clearly visible, typically in the header alongside your other essential business details: your full name, registered address, and legal form (e.g., Micro-Entreprise).

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between a SIREN and a SIRET number?

acceptedAnswer.text: This distinction identifies the 'who' versus the 'where' of your business. The SIREN is a 9-digit number that uniquely identifies your legal business entity for its entire life. The SIRET is a 14-digit number that identifies a specific physical establishment of that business; it is your 9-digit SIREN followed by a 5-digit location-specific code (NIC) and will change if your business moves.

mainEntity.name: How long does it take to get a SIRET number?

acceptedAnswer.text: After submitting a complete and error-free application through the INPI portal, the timeline is generally efficient. You can typically expect to receive your official SIREN and SIRET numbers from INSEE within 8 to 15 days, though it can sometimes take up to four weeks depending on administrative workload.

body.content: How to Get a SIREN/SIRET Number in France: The Global Professional's Playbook

This isn't just another guide to French bureaucracy. This is a strategic framework for you, the Global Professional, to compliantly launch your business in France and eliminate the "compliance anxiety" that often accompanies such a venture. For your Business-of-One, a SIREN/SIRET number isn't merely red tape—it's the key that unlocks high-value corporate clients and establishes your professional legitimacy.

The nine-digit SIREN is the lifelong identity card for your business, while the fourteen-digit SIRET identifies your specific place of operation. Obtaining these numbers through proper business registration is the foundational act that proves your business legally exists. Many talented professionals hesitate, fearing a labyrinth of paperwork and the risk of critical errors. This fear is valid, but manageable. Operating without these identifiers is considered undeclared work, carrying significant penalties and making it impossible to legally invoice clients.

This playbook is designed to counter that uncertainty, transforming the process from a source of stress into a clear, methodical plan. It is built for the expert—consultant, creative director, or tech specialist—who values precision and is ready to serve the European market as a French-registered micro-entrepreneur.
We will move you from pre-application uncertainty to post-activation confidence in three distinct stages:

items: **Stage 1: Your Pre-Flight Checklist.** We mitigate risks by ensuring your legal right to work, choice of business structure, and French address are in order *before* you start the official process.
**Stage 2: Flawless Execution.** A step-by-step walkthrough of the official INPI online portal, demystifying French terminology and highlighting common pitfalls to guarantee a smooth submission.

**Stage 3: Your Post-Activation Protocol.** We show you how to leverage your new numbers as a tool for building client trust, creating compliant invoices, and mastering your new responsibilities as a legitimate business entity.

body.content: Stage 1: Your Pre-Flight Checklist—Mitigating Risk Before You Apply

This initial phase is the most critical. The biggest mistakes—the ones that cause serious delays and frustration—happen before you even log into the application portal. This pre-flight checklist is your strategic defense against bureaucratic friction. By methodically aligning your legal status, business structure, and operational details, you guarantee a successful application on the first attempt.

items: **Confirm Your "Right to Work": The Non-Negotiable First Step.** Before investing a moment in the application, verify your legal foundation. For a non-EU Global Professional, this means holding a valid long-stay visa or residence permit (*Titre de Séjour*) that explicitly grants the right to work in a self-employed capacity. A visa marked *'Entrepreneur/Profession Libérale'* is typically required. Attempting to register without this authorization is a non-starter that will halt your progress immediately.

body.content: There are rules, it's very simple... what's your situation, how much are you making? Okay... then you're going to end up paying this, and this is 100% sure. There is no room for uncertainty.
body.author: Geraud Nayral, founder of French Tax Online

body.content: **Choose Your Legal Structure: Why "Micro-Entreprise" is Your Default.** For the vast majority of solo professionals, the *micro-entreprise* is the most direct and logical path. This structure is designed for simplicity, allowing you to pay social charges and income tax based on revenue, not complex profit calculations. To qualify, your annual turnover must remain below specific thresholds (for 2025: €188,700 for sales; €77,700 for services and liberal professions). Chartered Accountant Geraud Nayral, founder of French Tax Online, captures its value: "There are rules, it's very simple... what's your situation, how much are you making? Okay... then you're going to end up paying this, and this is 100% sure. There is no room for uncertainty." This certainty is precisely what makes the micro-entrepreneur status the ideal launchpad.

items: **Secure Your French Address (*Domiciliation*): The "Where" of Your Business.** You cannot register a business in France without a physical French address. This *domiciliation* is a legal requirement that will form a key part of your business identity, determining the final five digits of your 14-digit SIRET number. You have several valid options:
Your residential address: The simplest choice, provided your lease or property rules allow it.
A co-working space: Many offer business registration services as part of their membership.
A registered office service (*société de domiciliation*): These companies provide a professional address and mail-handling for a monthly fee, offering privacy and prestige.

body.content: **Define Your Business Activity (APE Code): The "What" That Shapes Your Future.** When you register, you must clearly describe your primary business activity in French. Based on this, the national statistics office (INSEE) will assign you an APE code (*Activité Principale Exercée*). This five-character code is more than a label; it influences which regulations apply to your business and can impact your social security contribution rates. For example, the APE code for an IT consultant (*62.02A - Conseil en systèmes et logiciels informatiques*) carries different implications than one for a translator. Be precise in your description to prevent future administrative headaches and ensure your business is correctly classified from day one.
Stage 2: Flawless Execution—Navigating the INPI Application with Confidence

With your legal status, structure, address, and activity defined, you are ready to engage with the official system. This stage moves from preparation to action, guiding you through the centralized online portal where your French business legally comes into existence. Think of this not as a bureaucratic hurdle, but as a structured process you can control.
Mastering the "Guichet unique": A Step-by-Step Walkthrough

All new business registration in France is handled through a single online portal called the *Guichet unique* (Single Window), managed by the INPI. This centralization simplifies the process. When you begin your application as a micro-entrepreneur, you will select "Entrepreneur individuel" as your business type.
As you navigate the French-language forms, you’ll encounter specific terms that require precise understanding.

header: Critical Insight for a Global Professional
rows: For a *micro-entreprise*, this is simply your own legal name. The business has no separate legal identity from you.
This is your optional, public-facing brand name. Declaring a *nom commercial* helps establish a distinct professional identity.
This is the detailed description of what your business does. Be consistent with the APE code you identified in Stage 1 to ensure correct classification.

body.content: Avoiding Common Application Errors
Simple documentary mistakes are the single biggest cause of delays. Have these key documents scanned, correctly formatted, and ready to upload to prevent your application from being flagged.

items: **Proof of Identity (*Pièce d'identité*):** A clear copy of your passport or, for non-EU nationals, your residence permit (*Titre de Séjour*) that explicitly allows self-employed work.
**Proof of Address (*Justificatif de domicile*):** A recent document (less than three months old) showing your French address, such as a utility bill or rental agreement.

**Declaration of No Criminal Conviction (*Attestation de non-condamnation*):** A signed declaration on your honor stating you have no convictions that would prevent you from managing a business. You simply download a template, fill in your details (including your parents' names, a standard feature known as *filiation*), and sign it.

body.content: Understanding the Timeline and Cost

The official registration of your business and the assignment of your SIREN and SIRET numbers by INSEE is **completely free**. Be wary of third-party websites that demand a fee for what is a free government procedure.

Once you submit your complete application via the INPI portal, the timeline is generally predictable. You can expect to receive your official SIREN and SIRET numbers within two to four weeks, allowing you to set realistic expectations with clients.
Stage 3: Your Post-Activation Protocol—Putting Your SIRET Number to Work

The official notification from INSEE delivering your SIREN and SIRET numbers is not the finish line; it's the starting gun. This is the moment you pivot from navigating bureaucracy to actively building your business. Your number is now a key asset, enabling you to operate professionally, build trust, and manage your legal obligations with confidence.
The Anatomy of a Bulletproof French Invoice
Your 14-digit SIRET number is a non-negotiable component of every invoice you issue. Its inclusion is a matter of legal compliance and a powerful signal of professionalism.
Here’s what a compliant French invoice must include:

rows: Your full name, followed by "EI" or "Entrepreneur Individuel," your trade name (if any), your business address, and, crucially, your **SIRET number**.
Your client’s name, address, and SIREN number if they are a business.
The word "*Facture*" (Invoice), a unique sequential invoice number, the issue date, and the date of service completion.
As a *micro-entrepreneur* likely not charging VAT, you must include the legal phrase: "*TVA non applicable, art. 293 B du CGI*." This prevents client confusion.
A clear due date (*délai de paiement*) and any penalties for late payment when working with professional clients.

body.content: Signaling Legitimacy to High-Value Clients

In the European corporate world, compliance is currency. Proactively providing your SIRET number in a proposal or contract immediately frames you as a serious, legitimate business entity. This simple act builds a foundation of trust before work begins, separating you from amateur freelancers and justifying the premium rates your expertise commands. For any large EU company, working with a registered freelancer is a matter of their own risk management; you are making their decision to hire you easier and safer.
Understanding Your New Compliance Rhythm
Receiving your SIRET number activates a predictable rhythm of administrative duties. Understanding these from the outset removes future anxiety.

items: **URSSAF Declarations:** You must declare your revenue to URSSAF, the social security collections body, either monthly or quarterly (as chosen during registration). **This is mandatory even if your income for a period is zero.** Missing a declaration can result in penalties.

**CFE Tax (*Cotisation Foncière des Entreprises*):** This is an annual local business tax. You are exempt in your first calendar year of business. In subsequent years, the amount is typically modest for freelancers and is not payable if your annual turnover is under €5,000.

body.content: Verifying Your Business Publicly

Your business is now part of the official public record. You—and your clients—can verify your details on the government-run *Annuaire des Entreprises* (Company Directory) by searching your name or SIREN number. We recommend you do this as soon as you receive your number to confirm your official listing is accurate and professional from day one.
The Final Step: From Compliant to Confident

Seeing your professional details reflected in the official *Annuaire des Entreprises* is the final piece of the puzzle. It’s the point where the abstract process of registration solidifies into a tangible reality: you are now officially, and publicly, a French business entity. This is a fundamental shift in your professional capacity.
This playbook was designed to engineer that exact outcome. Think back on the three stages not as bureaucratic hurdles, but as a deliberate strategic progression:

items: **Stage 1 was about mitigating risk.** You secured the legal right to work, chose the optimal structure, and established a physical anchor for your business.
**Stage 2 was about flawless execution.** You mastered the official process, armed yourself against common errors, and ensured a smooth registration without costly delays.

**Stage 3 was about activating your new asset.** You transformed your SIRET number from a mere requirement into a powerful tool for invoicing, building client trust, and managing your obligations with foresight.

body.content: The goal was never just to *get a SIREN/SIRET number*. The true objective was to dismantle the anxiety of the unknown and replace it with the steady confidence of a professional in complete control. You’ve moved from asking, "Am I doing this right?" to knowing, "This is how my business operates."

This confidence is your ultimate competitive advantage. For large corporations, a valid SIRET number is the absolute baseline for engagement. It proves you are a legitimate, accountable partner they can safely onboard and pay. You are no longer just a talented individual; you are the CEO of your own registered French company, with the official documentation to prove it.
Frequently Asked Questions
Confidence is built on clarity. Let's tackle the most pressing practical questions that arise as you step into this new role.

items:
**Can a foreigner get a SIRET number in France?**
    Absolutely. Obtaining a SIRET number is not restricted by nationality but is entirely dependent on your legal right to reside and work in France. You must first have the correct visa or residence permit (*Titre de Séjour*) that allows self-employment. Once your legal status is secure, the business registration process is identical to that for a French citizen.

**How much does it cost to get a SIRET number?**
    The assignment of your SIREN and SIRET numbers by INSEE is **completely free**. After you submit your business creation file through the official INPI portal, your numbers are issued automatically. Be cautious of third-party services that charge fees for this free, official procedure.

**What is the penalty for not having a SIRET number in France?**
    Operating a business without a SIRET number is considered undeclared work (*travail dissimulé*), a serious offense. Penalties can include substantial fines (up to €45,000 for an individual) and the required back-payment of all avoided social security contributions with penalties. Furthermore, you cannot legally issue a compliant invoice, which prevents you from getting paid by legitimate clients.

**Where do I put the SIRET number on an invoice?**
    Your 14-digit SIRET number is mandatory on every invoice. It must be clearly visible, typically in the header alongside your other essential business details: your full name, registered address, and legal form (e.g., *Micro-Entreprise*).

**What is the difference between a SIREN and a SIRET number?**
    This distinction identifies the "who" versus the "where" of your business.

rows: Uniquely identifies your legal business entity. You only ever have one.
It is assigned for the life of your business and never changes.
Identifies a specific physical establishment or address of that business.
It changes if your business moves, as the last five digits are tied to the location.

body.content: Your SIRET number is simply your 9-digit SIREN followed by a 5-digit location-specific code (NIC).

items:
**How long does it take to get a SIRET number?**
    After submitting a complete and error-free application through the INPI portal, the timeline is generally efficient. You can typically expect to receive your official SIREN and SIRET numbers from INSEE within 8 to 15 days, though it can sometimes take up to four weeks depending on administrative workload.
